title: A day between World Press Photo and Martin Parr
body:
In one day, I went to both the World Press Photo exhibition at Nieuwe Kerk and then to FOAM for the Martin Parr exhibition. It felt like I was experiencing the opposing ends of the photojournalistic spectrum.

The World Press Photo exhibition tours to over 100 countries, displaying the winners and the most compelling photojournalism and documentary photography from the World Press Photo Contest each year. Frequently featuring photographs of the world’s most challenging circumstances, they aim to incite action, educate and spark discourse on world events.

On the other hand, Martin Parr is a photographer known for his intimate anthropological projects capturing British seaside holidaymakers. His work documents people from across the class divide in Britain and the Western world through an often comedic, brightly coloured lens.

My experience at the World Press exhibition was harrowing. The majority of the photographs depicted suffering: war, tragedies, climate change, disease, repression, violence and poverty. Throughout the exhibition, visitors were prompted with questions about journalism and representation: How can journalism create impact? Do we need to see graphic imagery to understand what is happening in the world? But rather than provide answers to these questions, I felt like the exhibition complicated them further in my head.

There was a room with the winning photo from each year since 1955, and I had the impression that it was almost entirely photos of war and suffering. Why is the winning project every year most often one with the worst global tragedy?

If you look at the “positive” photos of the 2026 exhibition, they were still framed with themes of adversity. One photograph documented children from historically disadvantaged communities in Johannesburg gaining access to ballet training that was previously inaccessible under apartheid. It was an uplifting story, and yet it remained one about overcoming exclusion. Similar images of hope as a response to suffering appeared sporadically throughout the exhibition. I felt like it was asking us to admire resilience in the face of hardship, and I was missing an element of what we are all actually fighting for.

This is where the contrast with Martin Parr felt extremely pertinent. He doesn’t ask us to admire resilience. He doesn’t capture images of suffering, unless it’s a young mother suffering through her newborn’s screams on a sunny beach day. He captures life being lived. His photographs are also stylistically extremely different. World Press photographs seemed coated in colours of catastrophe: concrete rubble, dusty earth, smoke, blood and dim emergency lighting. Parr’s photographs felt aggressively alive in comparison. His signature style of vivid flash photography and artificially bright colours transforms the mundane into a spectacle.

Many say that Parr’s photography captures the ugliness of everyday life. Critics interpret his work as a commentary on consumerism, class, excess, and the absurdities of modern living. The Last Resort and Common Sense are filled with photographs of junk food, overcrowded beaches, souvenir shops, and the small indignities of leisure. But I found it rather beautiful. After a morning spent looking at photographs of people fighting to survive, his work reminded me partly what survival is for.

Going from World Press to Martin Parr felt like two very different ways of seeing the world. One was defined by what threatens humanity; the other by what persists despite it. The contrast stayed with me longer than I expected. I kept thinking about whether it’s actually necessary for World Press to depict hardship so consistently. How can we document what threatens humanity without losing sight of what makes humanity worth preserving? And where is the line between meaningful engagement and consuming suffering?

I don’t have the answers. But I’m left feeling like the question itself has shifted slightly: I’m not just wondering about what photojournalism shows, but what it does to us more generally.

And now that I’ve written this, I think I want to go and read Susan Sontag.
